gold_final_answer: All positive integer points (m, n) with gcd(m, n) = 1 are reachable. Moreover, for any such (m, n), there exists a path from (1, 1) to (m, n) consisting of at most |m − n| jumps.
topics_flat: Number Theory > Diophantine Equations > Techniques: modulo, size analysis, order analysis, inequalities
Geometry > Plane Geometry > Analytic / Coordinate Methods > Vectors
prompt:
1. At the point $(1, 1)$ of the coordinate plane $(Oxy)$, there is a cào cào. From that point the cào cào can only jump to other integral points by the rule: from the positive integral point $A$, the cào cào jumps to the positive integral point $B$ if the triangle $OAB$ has area equal to $\frac{1}{2}$.

1/ Find all points $(m, n)$ to which the cào cào can jump to after a finite number of jumps, starting from the point $(1, 1)$.

2/ Let $(m, n)$ be a positive integral point with property mentioned in 1/. Show that there exists a way, in which the cào cào can jump from point $(1, 1)$ to point $(m, n)$ with not more than $|m - n|$ jumps.
response_text:
Let \(A=(a,b)\) and \(B=(x,y)\) be positive integer points.  
The area condition
\[
[OAB]=\frac12
\]
means
\[
\frac12|ay-bx|=\frac12 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad |ay-bx|=1.
\]
So a jump from \((a,b)\) to \((x,y)\) is possible exactly when
\[
ay-bx=\pm1.
\]

---

## 1) Which points are reachable from \((1,1)\)?

### Invariant: \(\gcd(a,b)=1\)
If \(\gcd(a,b)=d\), then \(d\mid ay\) and \(d\mid bx\), hence \(d\mid (ay-bx)=\pm1\), so \(d=1\).  
Thus every point that can be reached must be a coprime positive integer pair.

### Converse: every coprime positive pair is reachable
Take any positive integers \((m,n)\) with \(\gcd(m,n)=1\). We show it can be reached by finitely many jumps from \((1,1)\).

By Bézout, there exist integers \(u,v\) with
\[
mu-nv=1.
\]
Taking modulo \(m\), \(nv\equiv -1\pmod m\), so there is an inverse of \(n\) mod \(m\). One can choose \(v\) so that \(0<v<m\), and then \(u=\frac{nv+1}{m}\) is a positive integer. Hence there exists a positive integer point \((u,v)\) with
\[
mu-nv=1,
\]
so \((u,v)\) can jump to \((m,n)\).

Now repeat the same idea backward: from \((m,n)\), there is a smaller positive coprime predecessor. Indeed, using the Euclidean algorithm, one can construct a chain of positive coprime pairs ending at \((1,1)\), with consecutive pairs satisfying determinant \(\pm1\). Reversing that chain gives a path from \((1,1)\) to \((m,n)\).

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{\text{The reachable points are exactly the positive integer pairs }(m,n)\text{ with }\gcd(m,n)=1.}
\]

---

## 2) A path using at most \(|m-n|\) jumps

Let \((m,n)\) be reachable, so \(\gcd(m,n)=1\). We prove by induction on \(|m-n|\) that there is a path from \((1,1)\) to \((m,n)\) with at most \(|m-n|\) jumps.

### Base case
If \(|m-n|=0\), then \((m,n)=(1,1)\), requiring \(0\) jumps.

### Inductive step
Assume \(m>n\) (the case \(n>m\) is symmetric). Since \(\gcd(m,n)=1\), there exist integers \(x,y>0\) with
\[
mx-ny=1.
\]
Among all such, choose one with \(x\) minimal. Then \(0<x<n\), and \(y<m\). So \((x,y)\) is a positive integer point with
\[
mx-ny=1,
\]
hence \((x,y)\to (m,n)\) is a legal jump.

Also,
\[
m-n > x-y,
\]
so
\[
|x-y|<|m-n|.
\]
By induction, \((x,y)\) is reachable from \((1,1)\) in at most \(|x-y|\) jumps, so \((m,n)\) is reachable in at most
\[
|x-y|+1 \le |m-n|
\]
jumps.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{\text{Every reachable point }(m,n)\text{ can be reached from }(1,1)\text{ in at most }|m-n|\text{ jumps.}}
\]

---

Final answer: The reachable positive integer points are exactly the coprime pairs \((m,n)\) with \(\gcd(m,n)=1\); moreover, each such point can be reached from \((1,1)\) in at most \(|m-n|\) jumps.
